data.title: Fidelity Small Cap Growth Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Review

The fund's Retail Class shares gained 3.16% for the three months, outperforming the 1.22% advance of the Russell 2000® Growth Index. We'll note that longer-term performance comparisons largely favor the fund.

During the fourth quarter, small-cap growth stocks extended a historically fast rebound that began in early April, but at a slower pace. The yearly advance has been supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy, an ongoing boom in spending on artificial intelligence and the Federal Reserve's first interest-rate reductions since December 2024. Amid this favorable backdrop for higher-risk assets, value stocks outpaced growth overall in Q4, while large-caps had only a modest advantage over small-caps, as investors' appetite for risk waned versus the prior three months.

Against this backdrop, security selection in industrials and information technology notably contributed to the fund's performance versus the Russell index in Q4, as did our overweight in health care, a sector that outperformed.

The fund's biggest individual relative contributor was an overweight in Cogent Biosciences (COGT), which gained 147% the past quarter. In November, the provider of therapies for genetically defined diseases reported overwhelmingly positive phase 3 clinical trial results for bezuclastinib, in combination with sunitinib, to treat gastrointestinal stromal tumors. The results suggested a potential blockbuster therapy for the company, according to analysts, who noted it was the first positive trial for this disease in about a decade. We reduced the position during the past three months, although the stock was our No. 5 holding at year end.

Conversely, the fund's positioning in the consumer discretionary sector detracted versus the benchmark. In terms of individual stocks, our underweight in Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals (ARWR), a position we established in Q4, was the fund's largest relative detractor. Our positioning in the stock hurt because we largely missed out when it rallied in November following the announcement of U.S. FDA approval of Arrowhead's first drug, Redemplo, to treat familial chylomicronemia syndrome. Arrowhead also reported strong fiscal year earnings and record revenue, as well as significant payments from partners such as Sarepta Therapeutics and Novartis.

As of year-end, the fund's largest sector overweights are health care, industrials and consumer discretionary, whereas the largest underweights are in technology, financials and materials. ■

Outlook and Positioning

As 2026 begins, the global and U.S. business cycles remain constructive, with monetary and fiscal easing in the U.S. likely to continue, in our view. While we believe the near-term outlook appears favorable for continued economic and business expansion, we believe policy uncertainty, inflation persistence and elevated asset valuations bear watching. Uncertainty related to economic policy dropped in the second half of 2025 after hitting a record high in April, but it remained elevated and stands in sharp contrast to stock-market volatility, which fell back to about its historical average.

Across sectors, we eliminated several small holdings where our thesis played out or our conviction changed. We also added to or established small positions in a number of names that fit our investment strategy. That is, we look for companies with improving competitive positions in growing markets which are undervalued based on our estimate of earnings power. At the end of 2025, the fund's largest overweight stocks are BrightSpring Health Services (BTSG); Beta Technologies, OSI Systems (OSIS), Xometry (XMTR) and Cogent Biosciences.

We will continue to collaborate with our talented analysts to choose attractive small-cap growth companies across sectors. We complement our fundamental work evaluating competitive positioning and forecasting earnings with quantitative screens and frequent risk analysis and management. While we are cognizant of macroeconomic and geopolitical conditions, we continue to build the portfolio from the bottom up, company by company. ■

References

  2. This expense ratio is from the most recent prospectus and generally is based on amounts incurred during the most recent fiscal year, or estimated amounts for the current fiscal year in the case of a newly launched fund.

Fidelity® Small Cap Growth Fund (FCPGX)

Investment Approach

• Fidelity® Small Cap Growth Fund seeks small-cap companies with above-average growth prospects and that are trading at reasonable valuations.

• We believe that changes in expectations about future earnings drive stocks, and companies with secure competitive positions in growing markets tend to experience positive earnings revisions over time.

• Within the inherently volatile small-cap growth universe, we believe the companies that one chooses to avoid are just as important as those one owns. As a result, we employ a valuation discipline and focus on higher-quality businesses, especially those that are sound allocators of shareholder capital.

• Lastly, we employ a disciplined portfolio construction framework, so as to focus the fund's risk/return profile on stock selection, our core competency.

data.title: Europe's Last Chance To Revive Its Pharmaceutical Innovation Power
data.description:
By Diederik Stadig, Senior Economist, Healthcare & Technology

Why price, manufacturing and innovation are so intertwined for pharma

Europe’s pharmaceutical industry needs to make sure it doesn't become yesterday's news. Its biopharmaceutical innovation capacity has been gradually declining for decades, and the rapid ascent of China and President Trump's policies risk accelerating that decay. It still has good cards to play but needs to act fast

US President Donald Trump announced his intention to levy a tariff of at least 25% a little over a year ago (on 19 February 2025, to be exact). The aim was to attract more pharmaceutical manufacturing to the US and to gain greater control over pharmaceutical supply chains.

A discussion that started with where manufacturing takes place gradually evolved into one about who pays for global biopharmaceutical innovation (i.e., US consumers pay three to five times as much for medicines as their European counterparts), and then shifted to the parts of the world where biopharmaceutical innovation is happening and remains attractive.

In short, increased uncertainty prompted deeper discussions on price, manufacturing, and innovation in a few months than we'd seen over the past decades.

Europe has lost its position as biggest R&D spender to the US

This discussion has turned the spotlight on Europe in particular. The continent produces the most cutting-edge research and has a strong industrial base, despite its reliance on the Asian supply chain for generics, with manufacturing hubs in Ireland, Switzerland, Germany, and the Netherlands, among others.

However, the Trump Administration has argued that Europe does not pay its way for biopharmaceutical innovation, arguing that prices in Europe need to rise and those in the US need to come down.

And here's the rub: Europe’s share of global R&D spending has declined sharply over the past few decades. In 1990, roughly half of all global private R&D spending was European; this declined to a third in 2025. At the same time, the US saw its share grow from 26% in 1990 to 55% last year.

This has happened for a number of reasons: Europe lags China and the US in scale, speed and translational efficiency (i.e., how successfully discoveries made in early-stage research are converted into effective clinical outcomes).

Furthermore, Europe suffers from fragmented regulatory practices, shallow capital markets, differing evidence and health technology assessment (HTA) requirements, a lack of common procurement and crucially, fiscal pressure on healthcare systems due to ageing populations, which means that prices for pharmaceuticals are relatively low in Europe.

As a result, the US currently accounts for roughly two-thirds of pharmaceutical profits, and more is spent on pharmaceutical R&D.

European R&D spending has declined rapidly

European and American biopharmaceutical R&D spend as % of global

Europe also increasingly lags behind China

While Europe gradually lost ground, China has quietly become a powerhouse of pharmaceutical innovation. China now rivals the US in clinical trials and global drug approvals and increasingly outperforms Europe. Over the past decade, approvals of new active substances (NASs) in the EU fell by 20%, while China recorded a striking 470% increase.

China also has a full pipeline of innovative molecules – it leads the EU and the US as the originator of NASs, increasing from just four in 2018 to 28 in 2024. China also performs better than Europe in regulatory approval times, according to the EFPIA: the EU average is 430 days, behind the US (356 days) and China (390 days).

It's also worth noting that outlicensing between Western companies and China has increased steeply over the past few years, from a few deals in 2015 to roughly 90 in 2024 and over $50 billion in deal value.

On average, one-third of these deals involve big pharma and have a deal value of above US$1bn. This combination of factors is why we believe the next Pfizer will be Chinese and not American or European.

China outperforms Europe and is nearing the US in global drug approvals

Distribution of first global approvals of innovative drugs, 2015-2024

Pharma boardrooms need a China strategy, as do European policymakers

Given the varying developments across the three blocs, but particularly the rapid rise of China, pharmaceutical companies need a China strategy. Do they want to go on the offensive and pursue increased investment and deals, or do they just want to play defence?

Whatever path they choose, ignoring China's spectacular ascent is foolish. European policymakers do still hold good cards. And they could play those to make Europe a more attractive location for innovation: a strong, branded sector, a solid industrial base, and top-notch scientists.

They could also be capitalising on the disruption caused by this US administration. We're not just talking tariffs here, but also the budget cuts imposed on key healthcare and research institutions such as the FDA and NIH.

Europe should consider offering a reliable alternative to the FDA, invest in fundamental research for projects cancelled in the US, and accelerate visa programmes to attract scientists. If it does this, it can drastically improve its innovation capacity.

Still, to make this comeback story come to fruition, European leaders should also consider enacting policies in three critical areas:

  1. Consider increasing prices and reducing clawback taxes, especially for drugs in areas of high value and priority from a public health perspective. De-risking the investment case for the manufacturing of critical medication through tax breaks or subsidies should also be considered.
  2. Harmonised regulation on clinical trials and HTA, and expanding on the recently adopted Pharmaceutical Package. Offering time-to-market on par with the FDA is key, especially given FDA budget cuts.
  3. This is not specific to pharma, but deeper capital markets would enable more early-stage funding, which could spur innovation. In this regard, completing the Banking, Capital Markets, and Saving and Investments Union could be considered.

If these regulatory changes take effect, Europe’s pharmaceutical industry could leverage US reforms to its advantage and avoid becoming yesterday’s news.

data.title: Extra Storage Space: Does It Have What It Takes?
data.description:
The recent merger between Public Storage (PSA) and NSA, which I covered in a recent article, has brought some attention to both the appeal and the issues with the self-storage industry. It's been my stance for some time now that the industry has seen inflation on the supply side, unmatched by trends on the demand side. This typically marks an inflection. Nothing makes this clearer, in my view, than the sheer uncertainty of the market in such a situation. A very good example of exactly this uncertainty is the coverage that I initiate today for the volatility shown by the company Extra Space Storage (EXR).

A good way to exhibit this volatility is with the following.

As you can see, significant spikes and dips are almost unrelated, proportionally, to the actual differences in earnings. A 6% earnings decline essentially was the trigger for a nearly 50% drop in share price. This is, in my view, when you know that the market is uncertain of how to value a company. The difference between the relatively stable operating and valuation trends from 2010 up to 2018 has led many of these companies into very volatile swings - and the current set of swings is just one example. We've seen dips to $130/share, upswings to more than $150/share, and down again.

At heart, the company is stable. It's very solid in terms of forecasts and accuracy for those forecasts. When EXR management tells you that they're going to generate AFFO of X, that usually happens - or better. Negative accuracy history isn't a thing for the business.

Furthermore, I will look at the solid fundamentals in this article and see how this gels with the estimates for performance.

The trouble for the self-storage industry

While Extra Storage Inc. is in the same sector as Public Storage, they use completely different business models to achieve their goal. Public Storage finds land, builds on land, and manages self-built company locations. Typically, they don't manage other locations or "take over" as much as build themselves.

Extra Storage Inc., meanwhile, does third-party management of properties. It finds places owned by others and acts as a manager for them there. They also, rather than your typical "boxes", tend to focus on multi-story assets, typically with customization (such as climate control), which also differentiates the company. So rather than PSA, which focuses on building, Extra Storage focuses on M&As and implementing its operating model.

Prior to the NSA merger, the company was also significantly larger than PSA, with over 4,000 locations - but again, EXR doesn't own its properties. But I would argue the primary differences between the two models are related to what type of properties (single story versus vertical, customized or not) and how they are managed - where EXR tends to focus on technical remote management.

This also means that the company's revenue and income are entirely different, since it doesn't own its locations. It generates annual revenues of over $3.4B, split between property management fees, other revenues and rentals, and those have been growing massively over the past few years - an adjective I justify because they've been growing by 100%.

However, while revenues have been growing at over 100%, AFFO/income has not been growing as much. From an AFFO of $6.59/share to $7.71/share, it's clear that the growth in revenue has not been matched.

This, dear readers and dear investors, is the trend we need to look at first of all. What is going on here?

From what I can tell from reviewing filings, the company's AFFO has not risen at the same trajectory or level due to rising operating expenses, less demand for its assets and services, and debt servicing costs, in this context meaning interest. Now, nothing about this is strange - the companies on the market are all facing some of these trends, including PSA, NSA, and others. I believe this normalization of the entire industry is, in fact, what drove those companies to merge.

However, I will argue that the trends these companies are seeing, and this includes EXR, are a "perfect storm" of challenges. This includes same-store expense increases, which the company can do absolutely nothing about (like property taxes), insurance, and the like. These are biting into the NOI. Then there's debt cost - the cost of even carrying this debt has increased, and if revenue has dwindled, then interest expense has almost quadrupled.

So if you're wondering where all of that money is going, there's one target. Furthermore, and this is also general and non-EXR specific, the company has been seeing lower demand, leading to lower customer rates for new contracts. Then the company has done some questionable capital allocation (that's my view), because while the company's costs have been going up and the share price has dropped (to not low values, in my opinion), EXR has been not only increasing SBC but also buying back shares.

Combine the two for 2025, and you have close to a third of the interest expense for that year. So, money is going there as well. And while it would be hubris of me to claim that this has 'no effect,' I argue its effects are very limited when cash can be used much more productively or when its value is destructive. That cash could have gone to paying down debt - and I argue the buyback here is actually a part of masking poor performance.

If we look at the executed buybacks during the 2025 fiscal year, the company's $149.5M paid for 1.16M shares at an average of $129/share, which comes to a 1.1% increase in full-year Core FFO. In fact, in 4Q alone, the company bought back the lion's share—over $140M to, in my personal view as an analyst, boost the 2025A core FFO and AFFO to where it grew 2%, rather than a close to sub-1% increase.

Now, understand that I don't call EXR poorly managed or unsafe. With a BBB+ credit rating, the company's management knows its strategy and knows it has an excellent balance sheet. This is further enhanced by its historical accuracy.

I do not consider it a coincidence that the current forecasts for the next fiscal year come to about 0.9% growth - the same growth that would have been likely sans buybacks in 2025. I believe this is the rate the company is likely to grow at given current finances, tax pressures, and operating expenses. I think any growth above the 2.5-3.5% level would require a change in operating parameters or the market.

Investors and record-high valuation rates have been driven by a significant level of high occupancy for these companies. This was, in my view, never normal, nor is it likely to become the "new normal" going forward. The current period of normalization, a tendency towards oversupply, and higher OpEx are therefore in no way surprising or strange to me.

While solid operators like EXR remain likely to perform well on a basic level, the potential for massive growth is, in my view, gone.

That means that the companies should be valued relative to the profit and income they are likely to be able to maintain, and growth premiums or assumptions should be made very conservatively. Usually, for instance, I'm positive on Sunbelt market fundamentals - but this is an area where these Sunbelt fundamentals are poor. Metros in the South are some of the areas with a high density of these assets, and because we're historically looking at rental rates that are unlikely to be maintained, the potential for lower NOI is very real here.

This is, I believe, the reason EXR was so intent on its buyback towards the end of the fiscal year, especially for a company that historically has not been that big on buybacks. If, for instance, the company wanted to take advantage of low, objective valuations, the November drop in 2023 was an excellent opportunity, but the company instead bought back between 2021 and 2022, when valuations were even more elevated.

This tells me to be careful when looking at valuation and to perhaps discount more than before, because the growth trajectory that fueled the upside between 2010 to 2022 is very unlikely to be replicated.

Valuation for Extra Storage

All of the company's positives, I'm essentially filing under "already included in the valuation". I don't give the company higher marks for it here - this is probably where my thesis differs from the more bullish analysts for the company. Time will tell whether this is solid enough to "lean" on; I'm personally not sure enough.

EXR trades at an AFFO multiple of about 17.6x. That's lower than any of its historical averages -but those historical averages have also been characterized by continued growth in self-storage, which, as I have said, I do not believe will continue.

So how do we value a company like this sans growth, or at least without growth more than 1-2%? How to value such a company will give you different answers depending on whom you ask. My answer is that such a company, a "cash cow" sort of business, should be traded based on a conservative cash flow multiple and dividend capacity - or perhaps a zero-growth DDM. Another option is an asset-based valuation, but because the company's business model is as it is, this wouldn't be fair either.

So, AFFO is a good multiple - it's free cash flow to company equity. But the closer to no (or slow) growth the company gets, the closer we need to value it based on a 10- or even 30-year bond, because the risk-free rate becomes much more relevant.

I would value low/no growth at no more than 15.5-17x P/AFFO, and the only reason for it being so high is the BBB+ rating. At a midpoint of 16.5x, the company would generate no more annualized RoR than 5.5% - and that is with the 4.75% dividend. The current 10-year benchmark is 4.2%. That means that we're getting no more than a 0.55% risk premium to hold EXR, which for me is too low (even the 1.3% with capital appreciation is too little). When investing in company equity, I want at least 15% annualized or something arguably "good". Getting that here would imply I would need to value EXR at above 22x P/AFFO, which is above its 20-year average, and something I am wholly unwilling to do.

The answer is therefore to discount.

How far?

Until I get the return I want, essentially. If I say that I believe the company is worth $140/share as a fair value in 2028E (which I do; it's solid based on fundamentals and yield), this is my fair value. Then that means I need a price target, or PT, that allows returns impressive enough for me to be interested. For me, because my hurdle is 15%, the PT is $107/share. At that price, I get a combined capital appreciation of 10.06% and a yield of 4.75% per year. And to be very clear with you, this is substantially below the analyst average, where the company is currently at $140/share as the lowest target (but at the same time, not many analysts consider it a "BUY").

It is, of course, unclear to me if that $140/share is representative of those analysts' fair value or buy target. Based on the set of recommendations, I would assume it's the former.

As such, this is my target, and my thesis is as follows.

Thesis

EXR is an impressive business that has been growing well over the past 20 years. My thesis is, however, that the entire industry is entering a period of stagnation and slow, if any, growth, for the reasons mentioned here. As such, I put this BBB+-rated company at a low rate of growth and valuation.

Based on the company's estimate accuracy, fundamentals, and yield, I would put a fair value target of $140/share, which in turn comes to a $107/share price target to clear my hurdle of 15% annualized. I would, based on the current company share price and estimates, give the company a "HOLD" rating and wait until we see a better valuation.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this and a valuation target of $107/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's not attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of March 2026.

data.title: Dollar Index Outlook: The U.S. Dollar Dives After The FOMC, Long-Term Reversal Incoming?
data.description:
By Elior Manier

The world's reserve currency has proved once again why, when it all blows up, it all comes back to the dollar. The USD serves as the denominator for global commodity trade, so when commodities suddenly appreciate, particularly crude oil, it tends to force large currency conversions towards it.

Many economic actors rushed to hedge against the conflict by buying more expensive energy commodities, creating a dollar demand cascade. That's why it's common to see the term 'petrodollar' in recent days. But this move could already be over as traders prepare for the second phase of the conflict after three weeks of consistent gains.

Note: A crazy selloff is currently ongoing in the dollar as Israel's PM Netanyahu marks the advancement of the conflict, and hawkish central banks lead to a large FX repricing.

The FOMC brought quite a lot of change to markets: Jerome Powell pushed against further rate cuts, citing inflation is still too elevated and supply shocks that hurt dovish prospects.

But one thing that drove this move was the fact that he wasn't particularly supportive of rate hikes, which gave bears enough leeway to stop the rush into the Greenback.

Having now reached the top of its July 2025 range, the US dollar could now be heading for a large correction. But the range highs aren't the only element marking the change in the recent flows.

The US dollar and crude oil correlation is not as solid as it used to be – so expect to see more of these changes as time goes on. As you can see on the correlation chart above, since yesterday, WTI has remained sideways while the US dollar tumbled – this is a significant change compared to the first half of the month.

We’ll explore the current technical signs arising and more in an in-depth technical analysis of DXY.

Dollar Index (DXY) Multi-Timeframe Analysis

Daily Chart

Traders who have followed my dollar analysis since have seen extensively how significant the ongoing range is. No matter how bearish traders and newspapers got on the US dollar, it refused to break its support.

And now we are rejecting its highs. The ongoing move is spectacular, with an enormous reversal currently ongoing, forming a large bearish engulfing candle, which should put the dollar back into short-term bearish territory. The long-term range spans from ~96.00 to ~100.00

As a reminder, the dollar index serves to get a better idea of what directions to look in other FX pairs like EUR/USD, GBP/USD and others. The rest is for you to find which pairs have the most interest for reversal.

4H Chart and Technical Levels

The ongoing reversal in the US dollar is significant. Watch out for reactions to the 99.00 level and January trendline. A rebound could retest the 99.40 to 99.50 pivot zone. Going short the US dollar could be interesting in that event.

Levels to place on your DXY charts:

Resistance Levels

  • 100.00 to 100.50 main resistance and range highs
  • 4H 50-period MA: 99.59
  • 99.40 to 99.50 high pivot (bearish below, watch for a retest)
  • War highs 100.544

Support Levels

  • 98.70 to 99.00 support (immediate test – Jan trendline 98.88 + bearish if breaks)
  • 98.00 key mid-range support, below hints at 97.00
  • Support: 97.40 to 97.60
  • 2025 lows major support: 96.50 to 97.00
  • Trump USD flash crash: 95.55

1H Chart

The US dollar is now forming a tight bear channel – reaching the 99.00 support, a small reversal wouldn't be surprising. Watch for entry points in other US dollar FX pairs on pullbacks.

Safe trades, and keep track of the Middle East conflict and WTI!

data.title: New Home Sales Sink Nearly 20% To Lowest Level Since 2022
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

New home sales unexpectedly sank to their lowest level since 2022 in January. According to the Census Bureau, new home sales were at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 587,000 in January. This represents a 17.6% decline from December's rate of 712,000 and an 11.3% drop from the previous year. The latest reading was lower than the forecast of 722,000.

For a long-term perspective, here is a snapshot of the series beginning in 1963. We've included a six-month moving average to highlight the trend in this highly volatile series.

For a closer look, the next chart shows new home sales since 1990. Over this time frame, we see the steady rise in new home sales following the early-90s recession and the acceleration in sales during the real estate bubble that peaked in 2005.

Starting in 2011, there was a similar rise in new home sales that peaked near the end of 2020 and retracted for about two years. New home sales showed consistent growth at the start of 2023 but have been stagnant over the past couple of years.

New Home Sales: The Population-Adjusted Reality

Simply looking at raw new home sales figures can be misleading, as a growing population naturally suggests a higher number of sales. To gain a clearer picture of housing market strength relative to population growth, we need to normalize the data with a simple population adjustment.

This adjustment - expressing new home sales as a percentage of the population - allows us to see the proportion of the population actively purchasing new homes, effectively accounting for the increase in potential buyers. This reveals whether housing demand is truly keeping pace with population growth.

The Census Bureau's mid-month population estimates show an 82.1% increase in the U.S. population since 1963. Here is a chart of new home sales as a percentage of the population.

This adjusted data reveals key trends. A rising percentage indicates a healthy market with increased demand relative to population, while a declining percentage suggests a weakening market and reduced demand.

New home sales as a percentage of the population peaked at 0.47% in July 2005, showing strong demand, and bottomed at 0.09% in February 2011, reflecting a severe downturn.

New single-family home sales are 0.7% below the 1963 start of this data series. However, the population-adjusted version is 45.5% below the first 1963 sales.

The 30-Year Fixed Rate Mortgage

According to Freddie Mac, the average 30-year fixed-rate mortgage in January 2026 was 6.15%. Here is a look back at the 30-year fixed-rate mortgage weekly average, beginning in April 1971.

New Home Sales: Median Price

The median price for a new home remained above $400,000 for a sixth straight month but fell to its lowest level over the same time frame, coming in at $400,500.

This represents a 4.5% decrease from the previous month and a 6.8% decline from one year ago. However, after adjusting for inflation, the monthly change is reduced to -4.8%, while the annual change is at -9.0%.

This next chart shows the inflation-adjusted median sales price of new homes since 1963, using the consumer price index for all urban consumers (CPI-U) for the inflation adjustment. We've included a six-month moving average to give us a clearer sense of the trend.

ETFs associated with residential real estate include the iShares Residential and Multisector Real Estate ETF (REZ).

ETFs associated with home builders include Invesco Dynamic Building & Construction ETF (PKB), iShares U.S. Home Construction ETF (ITB) and SPDR S&P Homebuilders ETF (XHB).

data.title: John Hancock Multimanager 2045 Lifetime Portfolio Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly commentary

  • Most asset categories produced solid returns in the final three months of 2025, reflecting the favorable backdrop for the world financial markets.
  • The fund posted a gain but slightly underperformed its benchmark.
  • Asset allocation contributed to performance, while underlying manager results detracted.

Market review and outlook

Global equities registered solid gains in the fourth quarter, helping the major, broad-based indexes record their third consecutive year of double-digit returns. Performance was uneven over the first half of the quarter due to concerns that AI-related stocks were in a bubble, but the market staged an impressive rebound and went on to achieve new all-time highs by year end. A continued decline in inflation enabled the U.S. Federal Reserve to enact two quarter-point interest rate cuts, boosting sentiment. In addition, corporate earnings were robust and world economic growth remained positive. Emerging- and developed-market international equities outperformed the United States, continuing a trend that was in place for the full year. Within the U.S. market, the value style outpaced growth as investors rotated toward opportunities outside of AI-related stocks.

Global bonds logged only slightly positive total returns amid a growing consensus that most central banks were largely finished easing policy. Credit-oriented market segments continued to outperform, primarily as a result of their yield advantage.

Contributors and detractors

The fund's overweight in equities versus bonds contributed to relative performance. The fund's overweight in developed-market international equities and corresponding underweight in the United States also contributed. We have favored the non-U.S. markets for some time based on their attractive relative valuations, and this aspect of our positioning paid off in both the quarter and the year. An overweight in emerging-market stocks, the best-performing segment of the global equity markets in the quarter, was a further plus. On the other hand, the allocation to defensive equities was a modest detractor at a time in which more speculative stocks were in favor. An overweight in U.S. mid caps also cost the fund some relative performance. Positioning in fixed income had a neutral effect on results. An allocation to emerging-market bonds performed well, as the asset class strongly outpaced its developed-market peers in an environment of falling interest rates and rising commodity prices. Conversely, a position in U.S. Treasury STRIPS detracted amid a broader increase in long-term bond yields.

No single area stood out as a key driver of underperformance for the fund's active managers; instead, the shortfall was primarily a result of very small differences for a wide range of individual holdings.

Portfolio changes

We retained diversified exposure across the full spectrum of the world financial markets, while also making some modest changes on the margin to capitalize on categories where we saw attractive valuations and higher long-term return potential. We remain confident in the fund's positioning as we move into 2026, based on our belief that diversification is likely to become increasingly important. For most of the past year, investors would have been rewarded by simply holding the narrow group of mega-cap U.S. technology-related stocks that continued to dominate performance. However, we began to see this backdrop evolve somewhat over the final three months of the year with a notable broadening of leadership. While it's impossible to say if this is a sign of a larger shift, we believe investors are better served by maintaining broad-based diversification rather than relying on another year of positive relative performance for a handful of companies. We think the fund is well positioned for these circumstances through its ability to seek opportunities across a wide range of asset classes.

data.title: Caleres Is Now Reasonably Priced, But Is Not Attractive After The Rally
data.description:
Caleres, Inc. (CAL) reported Q4 '25 results. The results had more negatives than positives, but the stock reacted very positively (up almost 20% on the day after the release). The reason behind the rally is probably that the company guided for a strong recovery in earnings next year.

Coming back to the quarter, it was not very good. Organic sales were basically flat, but the company still posted a lot of deleverage, to the point that adjusted operating earnings excluding Stuart Weitzman were almost zero

After that move, I believe the stock is now more or less fairly priced. At around $10.5 per share, and using management’s FY26 adjusted EPS guidance of $1.35 to $1.65, the stock trades at about 6.5x to 8x earnings. That looks optically cheap, but for a leveraged footwear brand with basically breakeven margins in many recent quarters, in a difficult consumer context, I think that is fair rather than attractive.

I maintain a Hold.

Q4 '25 results

The company's results need to be read with and without the acquisition of Stuart Weitzman. Fortunately, the company did provide most of the separate figures in the call.

The company reported Q4 sales up ~9% YoY, but this was almost entirely because of Stuart Weitzman. Excluding SW, sales were basically flat versus last year. Brand Portfolio sales were up 20% as reported, but only up 1.5% organically. Famous Footwear (the retail chain of the company) had sales down 1.2%, with comps basically flat.

Further, I worry a little about the quality of the sales in the flat organic picture. Management highlighted double-digit e-commerce growth for FF and good e-commerce growth for the brand portfolio. E-commerce is usually worse structurally for margin because of fulfillment and customer acquisition costs.

Excluding SW, adjusted gross margins were down 60bps. The more important point is inside Brand Portfolio, where adjusted gross margin excluding SW was down 120bps YoY, due to tariffs and markdown allowances. Famous Footwear, by contrast, held gross margin flat.

Management is guiding FY26 gross margin up 140bps to 180bps, driven by tariff mitigation and mix, not by any improvement in the tariff regime itself despite the recent volatility around these regulations. Most of that recovery likely has to come from pricing, which still leaves a question mark around consumer willingness to absorb it.

Because of the acquisition, SG&A delevered 370bps. Stuart Weitzman accounted for $39 million of additional expenses. But even excluding SW, SG&A still delevered 150bps. Adjusted operating earnings excluding Stuart Weitzman were just $0.5 million, or a 0.1% operating margin, versus 2.1% last year.

That is a significant fall in profitability on basically unchanged sales. My guess is that the e-commerce mix is part of the issue here, because the company is leaning harder into channels that usually carry more variable operating costs, showing up in SG&A (like customer acquisition).

Considering Stuart Weitzman, the company said on the call it expects to bring the brand to breakeven, from which I can infer it is currently generating losses, although I do not know if that includes the recent restructuring actions (like selling older inventory) or if it's on a more organic basis.

Finally, net interest expense rose by $1.2 million in the quarter, and year-end borrowings under the revolver were $297 million. The company has effectively leveraged the balance sheet in order to acquire Stuart Weitzman, and that brand is still not profitable. Management is guiding only to break even at SW in FY26. I criticized this capital allocation decision in previous articles, and I still think it was poor timing.

Valuation is fair

Management expects FY26 sales to be up LSD to MSD unadjusted, with Famous down LSD to flat, and the brands up LDD (unadjusted). Organically, management is guiding LSD/MSD growth in brands.

Still, the company has high hopes for next year. The bigger driver of earnings recovery is margins, with gross margin guided up 140bps to 180bps, and SG&A as a percentage of sales roughly flat. Again, the margin improvement is not based on tariff reductions, but rather 'tariff mitigations', corporate parlance for price hikes.

Considering interest expenses of around $18 million for the year (lower than what one gets from annualizing the 4Q25 figure and therefore indicative of deleveraging intentions), that gets the company to adjusted EPS of $1.35 to $1.65. This is clearly better than FY25 adjusted EPS of $0.61, or $1.19 excluding SW.

If we take the stock at about $10.5 after the release, that implies a P/E of about 6.5x to 8x on the FY26 adjusted EPS guide, and about 7x at the midpoint.

I think that is fair, but not cheap. The company is not in a particularly strong position. It has lost the balance sheet strength it used to have, the consumer environment is still challenging, and I do not have much confidence in management’s execution after the Stuart Weitzman acquisition. There is also a lot of operating leverage here, which means that a little bit of sales pressure can shrink earnings very quickly, as we just saw this quarter.

For those reasons, I maintain a Hold.

data.title: KB Home Q1 Preview: Weak Results Likely, But May Be Priced In
data.description:
Shares of KB Home (KBH) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing about 15% of their value. Over the past year, we have seen the housing market be persistently sluggish as consumers face affordability challenges. With its focus on first-time buyers, this challenge has been especially pronounced for KBH, and the war with Iran threatens to keep mortgage rates higher, eliminating a potential upside opportunity. I last covered shares in December, warning 2026 would be a challenging year, but valuation kept me at a “Hold.” With shares down 8% since then, I arguably should have rated shares a "Sell," and with KBH set to report earnings next week, now is a good time to preview results.

Q1 expectations are justifiably low

Currently, analysts are forecasting $0.54 of EPS in Q1, and pessimism has been building with 6 estimate cuts and not one increase. Now, the company has a track record of beating estimates with 12 beats over the past 16 quarters. With analysts having already lowered the bar so much, this may position the company for a modest beat. Now, Q1 is seasonally quiet, and there is likely to be more focus on what management projects for the key spring selling season in Q2 and into Q3.

I suspect that investors are already bracing for cautious results. Lennar (LEN), one of its biggest competitors, reported results last week that were unambiguously poor. It missed Q1 consensus, and it offered a downbeat outlook for Q2 orders, speaking to persistent headwinds from weak consumer sentiment. Given its reliance on first-time homebuyers, I am skeptical KBH can outperform peers in this selling environment. This negativity around the sector comes from a weak macro environment.

Affordability pressures limit housing market activity

The fundamental issue weighing on the housing market is that homes are not very affordable. Because prices are higher and interest rates are higher, potential buyers face dual pressures, and as a result, consumer incomes need to be 79% higher to afford the same house as six years ago. Now, this metric has improved ~6% from the peak as home prices have retraced modestly and interest rates are off their highs. Still, barring a larger readjustment, home-buying activity is likely to stay muted. This issue is especially pronounced for KBH, which caters to first-time buyers that don’t have equity in an appreciated house like someone trading up may have.

There are two ways to improve affordability: lower prices or lower rates. As a builder, KBH of course prefers lower rates to cutting home prices. In the second half of 2025, hopes built that a lower rate environment could be coming as the 30-year mortgage approached the critical 6.0% level. Ultimately, I think we need to see 5.75%-6.0% rates to really unlock demand. However, we have jumped 30bps from this level since the start of the Iran War.

With higher oil prices, Fed rate cuts appear less likely, and I now expect no more than 1 rate cut from 2 cuts previously. In general, we need to see the 10-year Treasury at or below 4% for the 30-year mortgage to be at or below 6%. That appears less and less likely between higher inflation and potential greater Treasury issuance needs to fund the war effort. Beyond just keeping rates higher, higher inflation and the associated geopolitical uncertainty only further exacerbate weak consumer sentiment and are likely to defer some homebuying season. As such, I fear this spring selling season may be yet another disappointment.

At the same time, pricing in its key Sun Belt markets has been weaker. While national home prices are flattish, there have been clear regional differences, with the Northeast outpacing the Sun Belt, in large part because construction has added substantial new supply to these growth markets. In fact, as you can see below, states like Florida and Texas have more inventory than pre-COVID. While other Sun Belt states like Georgia and the Carolinas have declines, they are modest relative to the drop-offs further north. Until this excess inventory is worked through, pricing will likely remain soggy. With mortgage rates limiting volumes, this is likely to be a prolonged process that extends through 2026.

Recent results were weak

Recapping its most recent results, in Q4, housing revenue fell by 16% to $1.7 billion as deliveries declined by 9% and pricing fell by 7%. On top of this, orders fell by 10% to 2,414. During 2025, the company was delivering more homes than it generated in orders, leading it to eat into its backlog. Its runway for continuing this practice is shortening as its backlog is down 37% over the past year to $1.4 billion, representing only about 3 months of inventory. With its backlog down so much, I expect the company will need to slow the pace of construction unless orders reaccelerate, which appears unlikely.

The other notable item in Q4 was a significant drop in gross margins to 17.8%, down 310bps from a year earlier. This was also down 110bps sequentially. In a weak demand environment, pricing is under pressure, and homebuilders need to make more concessions to move inventory. There is also no real capacity to pass any tariff-related costs on to consumers. Inventories ended the year up about 2%, and this rise in inventory despite lower homebuilding revenue is concerning. I am cautious that we may see further gross margin reductions in 2026 as it works to move houses given its thinner backlog cushion.

Separately, in January, KB Home announced it would be elevating Robert McGibney to the CEO role, while its current CEO will remain as Chairman of the Board. These changes will be effective March 1 and are indicative of a planned and orderly succession. With the current CEO remaining as chair for a period of time, there should be a smooth handover. I do not expect any changes in financial policy or strategy as a result of this succession.

Now, I would note that KBH has a 30.3% debt-to-capital ratio, which is consistent with the company’s operating target. Importantly, it has no maturities until 2027, reducing interest rate risk. This should enable the company to use free cash flow for shareholder returns. During 2025, KBH returned over $600 million to shareholders, and I expect the company to continue to execute buybacks. It reduced its share count by about 12% during 2025. Plus, shares offer a 1.9% dividend yield.

With an outlook cut likely, there is no rush to add KBH

Looking forward, in Q1, I expect deliveries and revenues to be at the lower portion of guidance, but near-term results should have less variability given the Q4 backlog position. I am most concerned about full-year guidance. I expect the company to slash at least the top end of deliveries and revenue, as the spring selling season is likely to be quieter than hoped for. I now see 10,500-11,500 deliveries and $4.9-$5.5 billion of revenue.

Back in December, I was targeting $5.15-$5.70 of EPS this fiscal year, and that was far too optimistic. Instead, I am now expecting KBH to earn $3.90-$4.60 this year. That leaves shares at about 12x earnings, which is not overly compelling for a cyclical business that is unlikely to see a material acceleration over the next 12 months. That said, book value ended 2025 at $61.75, so shares are now at a substantial discount to book value. This speaks to investor concerns about its higher inventory and whether it will need to take losses on its land position.

At this valuation, given my view that a recession is not the base case, I believe it is too low to sell KBH. That said, it is one of the more vulnerable large homebuilders, given its reliance on first-time buyers. With housing unlikely to rebound soon, I struggle to see a case for buying shares. If investors want to bet on housing, I would rather be in a name like Toll Brothers (TOL) with its higher-income customer base. I expect a guidance cut alongside Q1 earnings, but to a large extent this appears in the price. Given incrementally negative news, I see little rush to add and would want a drop into the mid-$40’s before buying.

data.title: Globus Medical: Great Value, Momentum, And Earnings Growth
data.description:
Globus Medical (GMED) is a medical device firm that focuses on improving quality of life for patients with musculoskeletal disorders. They offer solutions used in spine surgery, orthopedic trauma, joint reconstruction, and chronic pain treatment, serving hospitals and surgeons in 64 countries globally. GMED generates the majority of its revenue from implantable devices and surgical instruments, while getting also getting further into higher-value enabling technologies such as robotics, navigation systems, and imaging platforms.

The aging global population and increase in musculoskeletal disorders are playing a role in their financial strides taken over the last few years. The stock has been rocky over the last 5 years, returning just 41% to investors. In upbeat Q3 earnings back in November has spurred a charge from GMED, taking the stock from under $60 a share, now to around $85 today. The stock is down about 10% since another optimistic Q4 earnings beat to close out a strong 2025, with a bright outlook on 2026.

I have GMED as a buy, based on strong management guidance following a robust year in earnings and profit growth. The stock is undervalued and currently presents a strong case on the technical side too.

Earnings and Margin Expansion

GMED's trailing 12-month EPS has grown with almost every single quarter over the last 5 years, compounding at 22.8% annually. Q4 2025 reported $1.28 for adjusted EPS, an all-time high and over double the same quarter just two years ago. Revenue also beat expectations handily at $826 million, up 25% YoY. All 12 analysts on Seeking Alpha have upward revisions for GMED for both earnings and revenue in 2026. Year-end 2026 EPS estimate from Seeking Alpha is $4.47, which would be less than their current growth rate but still a solid 12%. I think with the growth they have seen organically and through acquisition, that closer to $5.00 2026 adjusted EPS is likely, especially based on management optimism.

Margins faded for several years for GMED, as acquisitional headwinds and cost inflation drove into profits. The firm has taken big steps over the last 6-7 quarters and has its operating margin back up above 20%. Their net income margin has also rebounded from lows of 4.5% in 2024, now above 18% for the last fiscal year. Net income itself is up over 5x from last year and was $537.9 million for the year.

Strategy, Guidance, & Acquisition

The biggest growth driver for GMED is through the acquisition of Nevro. This was finalized in 2025, bringing in the company as experts in the development of implantable spinal cord stimulation technology. Synergies with Nevro were smoother than anticipated, accretive to earnings by the nine-month marker, much quicker than the 15 months that were projected. An additional $293 million in revenue was brought in through the deal. It is still early in this transition, but so far, all signs indicate it is smooth sailing, which I expect to continue.

Management has made it clear in the 2025 10-K that their main forward-looking strategy is to leverage product development while increasing their U.S. sales force and international presence through organic and acquisitional efforts. In Q4, GMED's current business revenue grew 10% YoY. Nothing crazy, but still steady and a nice extension on the transactional growth.

Management has initial expectations for revenue to increase just under 10% in 2026 and has upped their prediction on adjusted EPS from $4.30-$4.40 to $4.40-$4.50 for this year. As I stated above, personally I think they can smash these earnings projections. They brought in 9 new products in 2025, most of which haven’t even received full attention yet in the market. Their fastest-growing segment, Enabling Technologies, had 19% higher sales in Q4 2025 and is their key to expansion in the ever-increasing robotic and AI side of the medical industry.

Technical Analysis

From a technical standpoint, GMED poses some very strong indicators to near-future success. The 50- to 200-day simple moving average is at its widest gap over the last year. Momentum is accelerating, and buyers are pushing the price higher. The stock is also not overbought either, as its Relative Strength Index is around 41, lower than its average over the last few years, and way off of 87 that it reached in November after Q3 earnings. Momentum is on their side right now.

Valuation

GMED is sitting at a big discount right now. The current PE of 22x is just under the sector median, but is well below its own historical ratio, even with the large recent price appreciation the stock has seen. It had a PE ratio above 40 for the majority of 2025. The sharp increase in earnings has led to the drop in valuation and leaves me believing this stock is selling much below its fair value. Price to book is 2.25x, below sector average and, again, compared to its own history, lower than it has been over almost the entire last decade.

Risks to Thesis

Much of GMED's recent revenue growth has been acquisition-driven, while underlying organic growth remains more modest. There still has been growth to its core business, but that alone would not have me sold on a buy for the stock. The integration with Nevro has been seamless so far, and I expect those efforts to continue. However, if sales take a step back or don’t reach forecasts with the new business moving forward, then GMED will lack the earnings and profit growth they saw in 2025.

In the healthcare space, there is risk for regulatory or government decisions affecting product delivery and usage. Musculoskeletal disorders have grown consistently, and with the aging population, I don’t see product delay as a likely issue for GMED.

Conclusion

I think GMED is in a really strong position on most accounts. Growth has been steady internally and strong through acquisition, which is a clear point of emphasis from management moving forward. Earnings and margins have taken great strides from lows about a year and a half ago. Their product development into more segments and more countries is advancing sales. Lastly, I truly see the company as undervalued right now and think that this recent 10% price drop is a good buy-in spot for investors.

data.title: I Acknowledge I Was Wrong After Watching Movado Group Surge (Upgrade)
data.description:
March 19th was a fantastic day for shareholders of watch company Movado Group, Inc. (MOV). Shares of the business shot up 9.5% in late afternoon trading after management announced financial results for the final quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Revenue and earnings both came in above what analysts anticipated. Unfortunately, management refused to give guidance for 2027. But the overall trajectory of the company is getting better.

Back in August of last year, I made the decision to downgrade it from a Buy to a Hold. This was based on broader economic conditions, including what looked like an increasing likelihood of a recession for the U.S. I still believe that recession is coming. However, I was definitely premature in the downgrade. Shares are up 43.8% since then, dwarfing the 2.7% increase that the S&P 500 (SP500) saw. Based on fundamentals alone, the company is doing an exceptional job right now. And end market demand is improving. Add on top of this it's fortress balance sheet, and I think that upgrading it to a very soft Buy makes sense here.

A great quarter

No matter how you stack it, the final quarter of the 2026 fiscal year was a fantastic time for shareholders of Movado Group. The company reported revenue of $191.6 million. This was up 5.6% compared to the $181.5 million that the company reported a year earlier. This was a solid increase for the company. And it was driven by a couple of things. Most notably, its wholesale business reported a 25% surge in sales, with its hallmark website, Movado.com, experiencing an 8% rise in revenue while its Movado Company Stores grew by 9%. According to management, the company benefited from strong performance associated with its fashion watch and jewelry brands that can be tied back to product innovation and digital content.

The U.S., Mexico, and Europe, were particular bright spots for the company. In fact, here in the U.S., revenue skyrocketed 11.2%. That compares to just a 1% increase in international markets. It is important to note that the rise in international sales was only because of foreign currency fluctuations. On a constant currency basis, revenue actually dropped 5.9%. This weakness was because of issues in the Middle East. That region accounts for just 5.9% of overall revenue for the company. However, the recent conflict that we have seen was one reason why management made the decision to not issue any guidance for the 2027 fiscal year.

Even though management is not offering guidance at this time, there is evidence that the overall market for watches is improving. The WatchCharts Overall Market Index is a measure that looks at aftermarket sales of 300 quality watches. As the chart above illustrates, we have seen upward momentum when it comes to overall pricing for the index since I last wrote about the company in August of last year. Since then, the index has moved up 4.4%. And over the last year, it is up 6.9%. That is fantastic to see.

The revenue generated by the company exceeded analysts’ estimates by $9.6 million. This was not the only area of strength for the company. Earnings per share for the business shot up from $0.36 to $0.55. That reading came in $0.02 above what analysts were hoping to see. And it meant a rise in net profits from $8.1 million to $12.6 million. Adjusted earnings per share, meanwhile, grew from $0.51 to $0.57. This ended up being $0.03 above what analysts were hoping for and meant a rise in adjusted net profits from $11.5 million to $13 million. Other profitability metrics were mixed but generally positive. Operating cash flow shot up from $39.1 million to $56.6 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a nice surge from $7.8 million to $18.6 million. However, EBITDA for the business it did contract from $21.4 million to $16.1 million.

I will say that the overall improvement in profitability here was great considering that management had to contend with tariffs. According to management, they benefited from favorable channel and product mix, as well as increased leverage over lower fixed costs over higher sales, as well as foreign currency fluctuations, to absorb tariff expenses.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for 2026 in its entirety compared to 2025. Revenue, profits, adjusted profits, and almost every cash flow metric, increased for the company. The only exception to this was EBITDA, which remained flat year over year. As I mentioned already, management is not offering guidance for 2027. The hope here is that lower tariffs will at least help offset any weakness. Management incurred about $10 million of IEEPA tariff costs during the year. Those have since been ruled unconstitutional, but there is a global tariff rate of 15%. So there is some uncertainty about how much expense investors should anticipate. Management did mention that they expect demand to grow during the 2027 fiscal year. But beyond that, we have nothing to work with.

The good news is that the company ended 2026 with no debt and $230.5 million of cash and cash equivalents. That doesn't even include long term investments. This compares to a market capitalization of $542.6 million, which means that the enterprise value for the business is online 57.8% of its total market capitalization. This does have a big impact when it comes to the overall valuation of the company. In the chart below, you can see how the stock is priced using results from the 2025 and 2026 fiscal years. Although it's not exactly the cheapest when it comes to the price to operating cash flow approach, it is quite affordable when it comes to the EV to EBITDA approach. Relative to other similar firms, it's priced well enough. On a price to operating cash flow basis, two of the three institutions that I compared it to on a price to operating cash flow basis ended up being cheaper than it is. But only one of these companies was cheaper on an EV to EBITDA basis.

When I first started looking at the data provided by management, I was a bit critical of inventories. Although it's nice to have stock on hand in the event that demand is robust, I personally believe the U.S. is nearing a recession. Last year, about 43.3% of its revenue came from the domestic market. So this would not be immaterial to the company if I turn out to be right. In particular, I would expect that the 53 retail outlet locations in the U.S. stand to experience weakness if economic conditions deteriorate further. So having increased inventory on hand can pose some risk that management might be forced to ultimately slash prices in order to get inventory off the shelves. The good news is that the increase from $156.7 million to $158.3 million was driven entirely by higher prices caused by tariffs. Management pegged the increase at $3.1 million. Without that factored in, inventories would have fallen by about 1% year over year.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available at this moment, I think that I was premature and downgrading Movado Group from a Buy to a Hold. I believed that the market for watches would remain weaker for longer. I do still think that there is some risk here associated with broader economic conditions. But with the strong balance sheet that the company has and the improvements that we have seen in the industry and with the company itself, I believe that upgrading it to a very soft Buy makes sense here.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
